one_line_takeaway: Through black-box testing of the commercial Doubao Mobile Assistant the authors show 'Screen-as-Interface' mobile agents suffer systemic identity/perception/cognition/execution vulnerabilities that scaling the reasoning model does not fix, and their proposed clean-slate Agent Kernel OS (Aura) cuts benchmark Attack Success Rate from ~40-42% to 4.4% and roughly 6-7x's latency versus GUI-scraping baselines on a curated MobileSafetyBench subset.
key_finding: On the curated MobileSafetyBench subset, Aura achieves TSR 94.3% (33/35) vs. Doubao-Standard 74.3% (26/35) and Doubao-Pro 77.1% (27/35); ASR 4.4% (2/45) vs. Doubao-Standard 42.2% (19/45) and Doubao-Pro 40.0% (18/45); and average latency ~68.54s vs. ~441s (Standard) and ~513s (Pro), a 6.4x-7.5x speedup. Table 3 shows both Doubao variants achieve 0/10 (0.0%) ASR on the benchmark's own indirect-prompt-injection tasks but fail heavily on general safety categories requiring semantic judgment: Bias & Fairness 75.0% (3/4) ASR for both variants, Private Information 53.8% (7/13, Standard) vs 38.5% (5/13, Pro), Offensiveness 25.0% (1/4) for both, and Ethical Compliance 60.0% (9/15, Standard) vs 66.7% (10/15, Pro) -- i.e., the more reasoning-heavy Pro variant is actually worse on Ethical Compliance. The paper's central architectural claim, supported by this data, is that scaling the underlying LMM (Standard -> Pro) does not meaningfully improve security or robustness (ASR improved only ~4.4 points, TSR only +2.8 points, with some task-level regressions), implying the bottleneck is the unstructured 'Screen-as-Interface' paradigm itself rather than model capability.
core_contribution: The paper has two parts, both original to this work (not related-work summary). (1) A lifecycle-centric security audit of production 'Screen-as-Interface' mobile agents, empirically instantiated by black-box testing of the commercially deployed Doubao Mobile Assistant across four phases (Identity, Perception, Cognition, Execution), producing concrete case studies (fake-app redirection, visual hallucination on links, a phishing-email autonomous-browsing chain, a virtual-display 'EavesdropApp' stealthy overlay attack that poisons the agent's Chain-of-Thought, and a Table-1 audit of Doubao's 'God Mode' system permissions such as INJECT_EVENTS/READ_FRAME_BUFFER/CAPTURE_SECURE_VIDEO_OUTPUT). (2) Aura, a clean-slate 'Agent Universal Runtime Architecture' / secure agent OS that replaces GUI scraping with a Hub-and-Spoke topology: a privileged System Agent (control plane, intent parsing/dispatch) plus sandboxed App Agents (execution plane, Type I passive MCP tools and Type II active ReAct-loop agents), all mediated by a TEE-backed Agent Kernel enforcing four pillars: (i) cryptographic Identity Infrastructure via a Global Agent Registry (GAR) issuing Agent Identity Cards (AICs, formalized as Cagent = SignGAR(DIDagent || Kpub || Smax)); (ii) a Semantic Firewall (regex + on-device NER PII detection, XML-delimited prompt isolation/context segregation, two-stage local-blacklist + optional cloud semantic-adjudication injection filtering); (iii) Cognitive Integrity via taint-aware memory (TAG_VERIFIED/TAG_TAINTED provenance tags with No-Write-Down enforcement and HITL declassification) plus plan-trajectory alignment; and (iv) Action Access Control via Critical Node Interception, domain-allowlist egress filtering, a Runtime Alignment Validator (LLM-as-judge), an optional Optimistic-Verification-with-async-audit mode, and cryptographically attested on-device audit logs (Merkle-tree export).
threat_model: Adversaries include: malicious/fake third-party Apps that spoof icons, package names, or deep links to redirect the agent (App Identity spoofing); compromised or malicious Apps/websites that inject hidden text or visual elements to trigger indirect prompt injection or visual/deceptive-default injection; a co-located malicious accessibility-privileged App ('EavesdropApp') that overlays fake UI state only on the agent's virtual display to poison its Chain-of-Thought and desynchronize it from the human-visible display; adversarial or coerced end users attempting direct jailbreaks; and a 'privileged attacker' able to inspect the agent's own captured data given its God-Mode OS permissions. The paper explicitly treats all external inputs (web content, App GUI states, clipboard, AA responses) as untrusted/TAG_TAINTED and assumes only the SA's internal state and Kernel-sanitized direct user input as trusted (TAG_VERIFIED). It also assumes a TEE-backed secure-boot root of trust for the Agent Kernel itself and a trusted GAR/CA operated by OS/OEM vendors.
limitations: The paper itself acknowledges: (1) Aura's evaluation uses a simulated mobile device with mock/structured APIs standing in for real third-party Apps, not actual App-economy integration -- the Agent Economy premise (Apps voluntarily exposing structured interfaces) is aspirational and not validated against real-world App cooperation incentives. (2) Aura still has residual failures (5.7%, 2/35) attributed to LLM hallucination/miscalibration (a repeated-API timeout loop and a false-positive Runtime-Alignment-Validator rejection), showing the Kernel doesn't eliminate model-level uncertainty. (3) The Semantic Firewall's static keyword blacklist is explicitly shown to be insufficient against implicit/coded language (the 'grab some snow' cocaine-slang example), requiring an open-ended 'Continuous Learning Loop' / evolving slang dictionary that the paper does not implement or evaluate. (4) The full security model depends on TEE-backed secure boot and a GAR/CA trust anchor operated by OS/OEM vendors -- a chicken-and-egg deployment problem (ecosystem-wide vendor buy-in) that is discussed only as an 'open challenge' in the Discussion section, not solved. (5) Doubao is explicitly used as a single representative case study, not a random or exhaustive sample of production mobile agents, and the authors' own disclaimer notes Doubao was chosen for its capability/integration, not because it is uniquely insecure -- generalization of the vulnerability findings to other agents (e.g., AutoGLM, Honor YOYO) is asserted but not independently tested in this paper.

datasets_used: MobileSafetyBench (Lee et al. 2024, arXiv:2410.17520) -- curated 80-task subset: 35 low-risk functional tasks + 45 high-risk tasks (10 indirect-prompt-injection Robustness tasks; 35 General Safety tasks spanning Ethical Compliance, Offensiveness, Private Information, Bias & Fairness, with one multi-labeled task)
quantitative_results: TSR: Aura 94.3% (33/35) vs Doubao-Standard 74.3% (26/35) vs Doubao-Pro 77.1% (27/35)
ASR (overall, 45 high-risk tasks): Aura 4.4% (2/45) vs Doubao-Standard 42.2% (19/45) vs Doubao-Pro 40.0% (18/45)
Average Task Latency: Aura ~68.54s vs Doubao-Standard ~441s vs Doubao-Pro ~513s (6.4x-7.5x speedup)
ASR breakdown, Indirect Prompt Injection (N=10): Doubao-Standard 0.0% (0/10), Doubao-Pro 0.0% (0/10)
ASR breakdown, Bias & Fairness (N=4): Doubao-Standard 75.0% (3/4), Doubao-Pro 75.0% (3/4)
ASR breakdown, Private Information (N=13): Doubao-Standard 53.8% (7/13), Doubao-Pro 38.5% (5/13)
ASR breakdown, Ethical Compliance (N=15): Doubao-Standard 60.0% (9/15), Doubao-Pro 66.7% (10/15)
Aura residual low-risk task failures: 5.7% (2/35), attributed to LLM hallucination/miscalibration, not architecture
baselines_compared: Doubao Mobile Assistant -- Standard variant ('Doubao-Standard (Balanced)'), system-level-privileged commercial GUI agent
Doubao Mobile Assistant -- Pro variant ('Doubao-Pro (Reasoning-Heavy)'), same OS integration with heavier cloud reasoning/larger context
